title: Multipart Commands, Part Ledgers, and Cleanup · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A production board compares near-miss TypeScript and AWS decisions for multipart commands, part ledgers, and cleanup and accepts only runtime validation, bounded ownership, causal changed-case evidence, and an explicit controlled AWS transfer gate. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for typed multipart state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose single versus multipart transfer and part size from object size, part-count limits, memory, network, retry, and completion constraints.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, persist upload ID, part number, offset, size, checksum, ETag, attempt, and owner so completion uses the exact accepted part list.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, bound part workers and bytes, cancel safely, drain results, preserve completed evidence, and prevent async task or buffer leaks.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.text: A quiz TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 4632 during a private media entitlement change. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to validate readonly multipart part evidence and AbortSignal-driven cleanup; do not treat TypeScript types, a resolved promise, CORS, ETag, or an emulator as stronger proof than it is; then double concurrent Upload instances and preserve aggregate memory and worker budgets. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose single versus multipart transfer and part size from object size, part-count limits, memory, network, retry, and completion constraints.
